title: Material-layer R-values (per layer)
text: The total R-value of a wall, roof, or floor is the sum of the R-values of its layers plus the interior and exterior air films. These per-layer R-values are drawn from the NREL openstudio-standards material dataset (BSD-3 license, attribution to NREL): half-inch gypsum board about R-0.45, five-eighths-inch plywood about R-0.75, eight-inch normalweight concrete about R-0.67, half-inch acoustic ceiling tile about R-1.27, plus roughly R-0.68 interior and R-0.17 exterior air film. The dominant layer is almost always the cavity or continuous insulation, whose R-value comes from the insulation table; these sheathing and finish layers add a little on top. Use these to understand how an assembly reaches its rated R-value.
steps: List the assembly layers from outside to inside.
Add each layer R-value plus the interior and exterior air films.
The cavity or continuous insulation layer dominates; its R comes from the insulation table.
Compare the total against the prescriptive minimum for the climate zone.
common_mistakes: Counting only the insulation and ignoring or double-counting air films.
Assuming sheathing and drywall add a large R-value (they add a little).
Ignoring thermal bridging through the studs, which lowers the whole-wall R.

jurisdiction_notes.fl: FL zones 1 to 2 need less cavity insulation; the layer math is the same.
jurisdiction_notes.nc: NC zones 3A to 5A need more cavity or continuous insulation to reach the higher minimums.

attribution: Layer R-values derived from NREL openstudio-standards (BSD-3 License).

title: How wall, roof, and floor assemblies reach their R-value
text: A typical wood-frame exterior wall is, from outside in, cladding, water-resistive barrier, sheathing, the stud cavity filled with insulation, and interior gypsum board. Its rated R-value is mostly the cavity insulation (for example R-13 to R-21) plus any continuous exterior insulation, with the sheathing, drywall, and air films adding a little. A vented attic roof reaches its R-value with insulation on the attic floor (for example R-38 to R-60 in colder zones). A framed floor over a crawlspace or unconditioned space is insulated in the joist bays. The assembly structure here follows the NREL openstudio-standards construction dataset (BSD-3, attribution to NREL), which lists each assembly as an ordered set of material layers. Confirm the required assembly R-value against the insulation table for the climate zone.

dimensions.value: cavity R-13 to R-21 + optional continuous R-5
attic-floor insulation R-30 to R-60 by zone
joist-bay insulation R-13 to R-30 by zone

steps: Identify the assembly type: wall, roof, or floor.
Determine the required assembly R-value for the climate zone from the insulation table.
Choose cavity plus continuous insulation to reach it, accounting for framing thermal bridging.
Verify the final assembly against the adopted energy code.
common_mistakes: Meeting the cavity R but ignoring continuous insulation the code requires.
Ignoring thermal bridging, so the whole-wall R falls short of the cavity R.
Compressing insulation, which reduces the installed R-value.

jurisdiction_notes.fl: FL emphasizes ceiling insulation and air sealing over high wall R-values.
jurisdiction_notes.nc: NC piedmont and mountain zones require higher wall and ceiling assemblies than the coast.

attribution: Assembly structure derived from NREL openstudio-standards (BSD-3 License).
